title: Lesson 308
transcript: Incident response, this is the process that we use to detect incidents and to try and reduce the impact of incidents that occur. Try and make sure that the business function, the system keeps running or we're able to restore it as quickly as possible. and to learn from our mistakes. So when something bad, an incident has occurred, how do we learn from that and try and make sure that that bad thing, that incident doesn't occur again? So these are overall the goals of incident response. Now, before we get into all the details of explaining exactly what the steps of instant response are, there's two terms that we have to understand clearly. And that's the difference between what's an event and what's an incident. An event is an observable occurrence. Me knocking on the table. That is an event. There's tons of events that are constantly happening in organizations. Do we care about the vast majority of events? Absolutely not. What we do care about are incidents. So what makes something not an event, but rather an incident? Well, the definition of an incident is that it's an event that has an adverse effect on the organization. So if an event occurs, you know, the CEO stubs their toe or a system goes down or something catches on fire, these are probably events, well, except for maybe the first one. These are events that we care about and we want to call them, give them a special name. We call them incidents. And it's these incidents that we want to be able to detect out of the sea of events that are constantly happening. We need to be able to detect which ones are in fact incidents and then have a well-defined process in place for effectively dealing with these incidents. So that's what incident response is all about. This next slide here shows you some examples of how you would go about detecting if an incident has occurred. You can use things like your IPS IDS, your intrusion detection, intrusion prevention systems. You can use your anti-malware systems, often your SIEM or SIM systems. Could be cameras, could be guards. These are all examples of how you could detect that something was not an event, but actually an incident. And what are some examples of actual incidents? Well, it could be hackers, could be a terrorist attack, could be a malicious insider, could be employee error. These are all examples of incidents. So now let's walk through the incident response process. In order to have an effective incident response process, the first thing you need to do is you actually have to prepare ahead of time. If the first time that you have an incident is the first time you say, hey, I wonder how we're going to respond to this and then try and figure it out, it's probably not going to go very well. So the first thing you have to do, create an incident response process, assign people with the right responsibility, accountability, start to create the processes of how you're going to detect stuff, how you're going to respond, what you're going to do in different types of scenarios. You know, what happens if it occurs in this physical location, in this data center? What if it's this type of system? Do some preparation. I want you to listen very carefully to what I'm about to say here. It's not uncommon on the exam to be asked what the first step of instant response is. And while you obviously have to have done preparation, the answer you're going to look for is, in fact, detection. If you see any questions asking you what's, you know, put the steps in order for instant response or what's the first step, always look for detection as the first step. It's the most important. If you can't detect that an incident occurs, you can't have an instant response process. You can't do all the things that you're supposed to do. So the first most important step in instant response is detection. This is how we actually detect if something bad has happened. As soon as you've detected something, the very first thing you want to do is response. And this is where you activate your incident response team. And one of the very first things they're going to do as part of the response is do an impact assessment. They're going to figure out how big of a deal is this impact. How long is it going to take to cover? Who needs to be involved in this response? And remember that, okay? Because this is going to come up later when we talk more about BCP and DRP. This impact assessment is one of the very first things that the incident response team does. Next is mitigation. Mitigation is all about trying to contain the damage, try and minimize the impact on the organization. And so if this was a fire, for instance, the mitigation phase or step in incident response would be activating the fire suppression system, put out the fire. Or if it was a virus outbreak, the containment might be to, if it was a worm, for instance, spreading quickly across your network, you might actually start to unplug systems to try and contain, minimize the damage. Next step is reporting. And you actually do reporting throughout this entire process. But there's one important thing we have to talk about related to reporting. And that is that there's often a lot of stakeholders who want updates, especially if it's a major incident. You're gonna be telling your senior management, You're going to be telling the system owner. You're going to be telling your customers. You might be telling the media. You might have to pull in HR and legal and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. There can potentially be many, many stakeholders. And one of the problems that can occur is that if you've got tens, hundreds of people that are like constantly bugging the incident response team to say, hey, what's going on? Where are we? What's happening? What are we doing? The incident response team is not going to be able to focus on what they need to be doing, which is responding to the incident. So as part of reporting, you need to figure out who are all the relevant stakeholders that need updates, at what cadence, and importantly, designate one person on the incident response team who's responsible for communicating with everyone else, and then leave the rest of the team focused on actually doing what they should be doing, which is responding to the incident. Next step is recovery. And this is where we start to try and return things to normal. So in the virus outbreak example that I gave, this is where you'd start to remove the malware, the worm from the systems, where you'd start to plug these systems back into the network. In the fire example I talked about, this is where you'd clean up all the water and fire damage from the firing that had occurred, start putting walls back up, reinstalling systems, electrical, et cetera. So recovery is about getting back to normal. As part of this is remediation. We don't want to just return to exactly the state we were at before because that allowed us to have a malware outbreak or a fire. So a part of remediation is to say, how do we try and prevent this from occurring again? What improvements can we make to our processes, to our controls to try and prevent reoccurrence? And the final piece of this is where you do that soul searching. It's called lessons learned. This is where you ask yourself, how did I get here? What went wrong? Why am I even doing this? It's to figure out how do you improve the process. And so there you go, those are all the steps of instant response. And remember, what's the very first and most important step? Detection. One final piece I'll layer over this is that you're sometimes asked to categorize these controls. And this categorization kind of comes from the military and from the medical field if you ever worked there. The first two steps you can group together and you call it triage. So detection response is the initial triage. Figure out how bad it is, how badly is the person bleeding, whatever. Can you treat them there in the field or you have to bring them back to some hospital in Germany or something. Next step is the next categorization is action and investigation. And this is where you do your mitigation and your reporting. And then the final piece is recovery. And that groups together your recovery, your remediation, and your lessons learned. So if you ever see that categorization, triage, action investigation and recovery, that's how it lines up to the different incident response steps that we've talked about.
